content: /// Open the tree, project the root through `RootSyntax`, and
guard let only = expressions.first, expressions.count == 1 else {
mutating func evaluate(_ expression: ExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue {
/// Parse the integer literal directly from the token's UTF-8 bytes.
/// `withTextUTF8` exposes the token's bytes through a borrowed
/// buffer pointer — there is no `String` allocation per token.
/// `parseInt64` accumulates digits with overflow checks; on the
/// rare error path we materialize `text` for the diagnostic.
private mutating func evaluateInteger(_ expression: IntegerExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue {
let parsed: Int64? = try token.withTextUTF8 { bytes in
/// Reals fall back to `Double(_:)` because the stdlib has no
/// from-bytes parser. We still avoid `token.makeString()` on the
/// happy path by going through `withTextUTF8` and decoding the
private mutating func evaluateReal(_ expression: RealExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue {
let text = try token.withTextUTF8 { bytes in
guard let value = Double(text), value.isFinite else {
// Compound evaluators filled in by the next step.
private mutating func evaluateUnary(_ expression: UnaryExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue { fatalError() }
private mutating func evaluateBinary(_ expression: BinaryExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue { fatalError() }
private mutating func evaluateGroup(_ expression: GroupExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue { fatalError() }
private mutating func evaluateRoundCall(_ expression: RoundCallExprSyntax) throws -> CalculatorValue { fatalError() }
internal func parseInt64(asciiDigits bytes: UnsafeBufferPointer<UInt8>) -> Int64? {
guard byte >= 0x30, byte <= 0x39 else { return nil }

whole document each time is wasteful when most subtrees did
not change.

doc://org.cambium.Cambium/tutorials/Camb.title: Ask the oracle for a reusable subtree

abstract.text: Parallel parsing splits work across worker tasks: each
worker parses an independent subtree concurrently, then a
master builder splices the worker results into one logical
tree. The correctness-critical question is how the workers
share token-text vocabulary and the green-node namespace.

abstract.text: A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen
through
cursors handed to a closure, and source
text is exposed as a chunk-streaming view rather than a
materialized
With the kind enum in place, you can write a recursive-descent
parser that emits into a
. The
builder accepts a stream of
calls and produces a green tree whose structure
mirrors the calls one-for-one.
Cambium’s CST stores everything as homogeneous nodes tagged
with a kind. That uniformity is what makes editing and reuse
cheap, but it leaves consumers to write
everywhere they want kind-specific behavior.
The witnesses you built in Tutorial 7 (replacement) and
Tutorial 8 (reparse) exist to make analysis caches
. Without translation every keystroke would invalidate
every memoized value — defeating the whole point of an
incremental tool.
With the typed AST in place from Tutorial 5, an evaluator
becomes a recursive function that pattern-matches on
. Each kind has a closed structure — its operands
and operator are addressable through the typed accessors —
so the evaluator stays small and total.
Every Cambium grammar starts with a kind enumeration: one case per
token and one per node the parser can produce. The kind enum
becomes the
associated type of a
conformance, and Cambium specializes its generic types around that
language to make tree storage compact and traversal type-safe.
Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.

doc://org.cambium.Cambium/tutorials/Camb.title: Finish the parser and obtain a tree

abstract.text: A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen
through
cursors handed to a closure, and source
text is exposed as a chunk-streaming view rather than a
materialized
Cambium is a Swift-native concrete syntax tree library. This
tutorial series walks you through building a small but
production-shaped calculator language end-to-end: a recursive-
descent parser that produces a lossless tree, a typed AST overlay,
an evaluator with memoized analysis, persistent edits with
identity-preserving witnesses, an incremental reparse loop, fork-
join evaluation across structured-concurrent tasks, parallel
parsing with externalized interning, and on-disk serialization.
A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen
through
cursors handed to a closure, and source
text is exposed as a chunk-streaming view rather than a
materialized

is
. Cambium’s red
layer realizes lazily under lock-free atomic slots, so two
tasks descending into different siblings of the same parent
never observe inconsistent state. The typed AST overlays are
also
, so passing a sub-expression to a
child task is just an argument pass.
Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.
